did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel deep learning-based approach for melanoma detection. Specifically, it implements a hyper-tuned EfficientNet-B1 model (termed Hyper-EfficientNet-B1) with additional hidden and fully connected layers for classifying skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The study compares this against ResNet50 and DenseNet121, achieving 86% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a novel deep learning model (Hyper-EfficientNet-B1) for melanoma detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology (data augmentation, class weighting, 10-fold cross-validation), results (86% accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score comparisons with ResNet50 and DenseNet121), and is published in the proceedings of the 4th International Conference on Computing and Information Technology (ICCIT).

title: A Novel Deep Learning based Melanoma Detection Technique using Skin Lesion
journal: 2025 4th International Conference on Computing and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel deep learning approach for melanoma detection using dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. To address class imbalance, the authors implemented a hyper-tuned EfficientNet-B1 model, termed Hyper-EfficientNet-B1, which incorporates additional hidden and fully connected layers along with class weightage. The model was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation and compared against ResNet50 and DenseNet121 architectures. The proposed Hyper-EfficientNet-B1 achieved an accuracy of 86.26%, outperforming ResNet50 (81%) and DenseNet121 (82%). The results demonstrate that the modified architecture effectively handles complex image features and data imbalance for accurate skin cancer classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (7 classes including Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs malignant or specific lesion types) using a Softmax activation function in the output layer. The paper explicitly states the process works as 'multi classification for the cancer detection' and evaluates performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images provided by ISIC, containing over 10,000 images collected from various populations using different modalities. It is used as the primary data source for training and evaluating the deep learning models for melanoma detection.

use.training: Used for training the Hyper-EfficientNet-B1, ResNet50, and DenseNet121 models. The study mentions using a 90/10 split for train/test data in the evaluation phase (Table I caption), though the methodology section also references 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation to find unbiased results.
use.testing: Used for testing with a 10% split (as per Table I caption: 'Test = 10') to evaluate precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To find unbiased results for accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.
To evaluate classification results, analyze true/false positive and negative rates, and understand error types across classes.
To calculate precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score without differentiating between classes, providing an unbiased average compared to weighted average.
To calculate precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score, noting that it is biased because it relies on the sample size of higher classes in the dataset.
To mitigate data imbalance by calculating weightage for each class, reducing the count of classes with higher data to not more than 4-folds compared to classes with lower data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between Hyper-EfficientNet-B1, ResNet50, and DenseNet121 were based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics derived from confusion matrices and 10-fold cross-validation, without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (ISIC).notes: The Hyper-EfficientNet-B1 model outperformed ResNet50 and DenseNet121 in precision, accuracy, recall, and F1-score. The paper notes that Weighted Average is biased due to reliance on sample size of higher classes, while Macro Average does not differentiate between classes. Confusion matrices were used to analyze errors by class, with the proposed model showing fewer errors in the top 3 dominant classes compared to baselines.

architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (Input vector size equivalent to class count mentioned for output layer; input image dimensions not specified).

Class weighting: Calculated weightage for each class to mitigate data imbalance (reducing count of higher data classes to not more than 4-folds compared to lower data classes)
Data chunking: Data chunked with not more than 3-K folds (mentioned in methodology section)
Normalization: Batch-Normalization added to normalize activations of the previous layer for each batch of data

conclusions: The proposed Hyper-EfficientNet-B1 model achieved an accuracy of 86.06%, outperforming ResNet50 (81%) and DenseNet121 (82%) in melanoma detection using the HAM10000 dataset.
The Hyper-EfficientNet-B1 model demonstrated superior performance compared to baseline models in terms of precision (88), recall (86), and F1-score (87).

Class weighting was an effective strategy for mitigating data imbalance, specifically by reducing the count of classes with higher data to not more than 4-folds compared to classes with lower data counts.

Standard data augmentation techniques (rotation, zoom, rescale) were found to increase similarity indices between edges of different classes, resulting in a biased model; therefore, class weighting was preferred over augmentation for handling imbalance in this context.

The modified architecture, which includes additional hidden and fully connected layers along with dropout and batch normalization, effectively handles complex image features and data imbalance for accurate skin cancer classification.

The study acknowledges that classification accuracy can be misleading in multi-class or imbalanced datasets, necessitating the use of confusion matrices and macro/weighted averages for comprehensive evaluation.
The proposed model showed fewer errors in the top 3 dominant classes compared to ResNet50 and DenseNet121, indicating better generalization for the most prevalent lesion types.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant population bias (predominantly Caucasian data) and lack of diversity, limiting generalizability to other skin types and demographics.

The paper does not report an external validation on a separate, independent dataset; results are derived solely from internal cross-validation on the training data, raising concerns about overfitting and real-world clinical applicability.

The authors explicitly state that standard data augmentation (rotation, zoom, rescale) resulted in an 'extremely biased model' by increasing similarity indices between edges of different classes, leading them to rely on class weighting instead.

The class weighting strategy is limited to reducing the count of higher-data classes to 'not more than 4-folds' compared to lower-data classes, which may be insufficient for severe class imbalances in melanoma detection.
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters such as the optimizer type, learning rate schedule details, or initial weights, making reproducibility difficult.

The input image dimensions and preprocessing steps (e.g., resizing, normalization ranges) are not explicitly reported, only that the input vector size was equivalent to the class count for the output layer.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance differences between Hyper-EfficientNet-B1 and the baseline models (ResNet50, DenseNet121).

The ground truth for the HAM10000 dataset is heterogeneous, relying on histopathology for only some lesions, while others rely on follow-up, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy, introducing potential label noise.

The reported accuracy of 86.06% is modest for a multi-class problem with 7 classes, and the paper acknowledges that accuracy can be misleading in imbalanced datasets, though it does not provide per-class metrics in the main text (only confusion matrices are referenced).
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICCIT 2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to top-tier medical AI journals.
